## Goal
Measure real capability usage and suggest minimal capability sets per zone/connector.

## Why
- Reduces over-provisioned capabilities.
- Enables least-privilege hardening over time.
- Provides actionable operator insights.

## Scope
- Usage telemetry collection and aggregation.
- Analyzer to recommend minimal capability sets.
- CLI reports + export for automation.

## Deliverables
- CapabilityUsage event schema and aggregation store.
- Recommendation engine with explainable rules.
- CLI reports (usage, unused, suggested reductions).
- Unit/integration tests + E2E script with structured logs.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-3ijc","title":"fcp.redis: Redis Cache and Pub/Sub Connector","description":"# fcp.redis - Redis Cache and Pub/Sub Connector


## Goal
Provide an FCP2-compliant Redis connector for cache/storage, pub/sub, and stream operations with strict capability gating and bounded resource use.

## External surface (NetworkConstraints)
Default deny.
- Network egress MUST be restricted per-operation via manifest `NetworkConstraints` (defined in the connector’s manifest/cap map bead if present).
- Allow only the configured service host(s); HTTPS-only where applicable; deny redirects by default.
- Bound timeouts + response sizes; treat all remote responses as untrusted input; never leak secrets/PII in logs or errors.
- For DB/queue connectors, constrain raw TCP connects to explicit host:port only; deny private ranges unless explicitly allowed by policy.

## Overview
Redis connector for caching, key-value storage, pub/sub messaging, and data structure operations.

## Manifest archetypes (closed set)
- `storage`
- `operational`
- `bidirectional`

> Interaction patterns (non-archetype): Request/Response + Queue/Pub-Sub

## Key Operations

### String Operations
- `redis.get` - Get value
- `redis.set` - Set value (with TTL, NX/XX)
- `redis.mget/mset` - Multiple get/set
- `redis.incr/decr` - Atomic increment/decrement
- `redis.append` - Append to string
- `redis.getrange/setrange` - Substring operations

### Hash Operations
- `redis.hget/hset` - Single field get/set
- `redis.hmget/hmset` - Multiple fields
- `redis.hgetall` - Get all fields
- `redis.hdel` - Delete fields
- `redis.hincrby` - Increment field

### List Operations
- `redis.lpush/rpush` - Push to list
- `redis.lpop/rpop` - Pop from list
- `redis.lrange` - Get range
- `redis.llen` - List length
- `redis.lindex` - Get by index
- `redis.blpop/brpop` - Blocking pop

### Set Operations
- `redis.sadd/srem` - Add/remove members
- `redis.smembers` - Get all members
- `redis.sismember` - Check membership
- `redis.sinter/sunion/sdiff` - Set operations

### Sorted Set Operations
- `redis.zadd/zrem` - Add/remove members
- `redis.zrange/zrevrange` - Get by rank
- `redis.zscore` - Get score
- `redis.zrangebyscore` - Get by score range
- `redis.zincrby` - Increment score

### Pub/Sub Operations
- `redis.publish` - Publish message
- `redis.subscribe` - Subscribe to channels
- `redis.psubscribe` - Pattern subscribe
- `redis.unsubscribe` - Unsubscribe

### Stream Operations
- `redis.xadd` - Add to stream
- `redis.xread` - Read from stream
- `redis.xrange` - Read range
- `redis.xgroup` - Consumer groups
- `redis.xreadgroup` - Group read

### Key Operations
- `redis.del` - Delete keys
- `redis.exists` - Check existence
- `redis.expire/ttl` - TTL management
- `redis.keys/scan` - Key enumeration
- `redis.type` - Get key type

### Transaction Operations
- `redis.multi/exec` - Transactions
- `redis.watch` - Optimistic locking

## Providers Supported
- Redis OSS
- Redis Enterprise
- AWS ElastiCache
- Azure Cache for Redis
- Upstash
- Redis Cloud

## Connection Management
- Connection pooling
- Cluster mode support
- Sentinel support
- TLS connections

## Authentication
- Password authentication
- ACL users (Redis 6+)
- TLS client certificates

## Zone Architecture

<!-- fcp2-single-zone-note -->
**FCP2 single-zone binding note:** A connector instance binds to exactly one `ZoneId` for its entire lifetime.
The *"zones"* described in this section are a **recommended deployment pattern** (run separate connector instances in separate zones with different capability grants/policies) and/or conceptual risk tiers.
The connector MUST NOT multiplex multiple zones internally.

```
Zone 0 (Public):     N/A
Zone 1 (Authorized): Read operations
Zone 2 (Private):    Write operations
Zone 3 (Sensitive):  Admin operations, credentials
```

## Dependencies
- fcp-core, fcp-ratelimit, fcp-testkit, fcp-telemetry

## Acceptance Criteria
- The functionality described in this bead is implemented with typed schemas + validation (no unstructured “stringly” interfaces).
- Errors map to the FCP error taxonomy with correct retry semantics and no secret/PII leakage.
- Mock-only unit/integration tests cover success paths and common failures and emit structured JSON logs per `flywheel_connectors-1n78.35`.

## Goal
End-to-end script validating preview, apply, and rollback workflow.

## Script
- `e2e/policy_bundle_flow.sh` with structured JSON logs.
- Steps:
  1) Create bundle from baseline.
  2) Diff + risk summary.
  3) Preview with sample requests.
  4) Apply bundle and verify allow/deny changes.
  5) Roll back to prior bundle and verify restoration.

## Logging
- Emit JSON per step: {stage, bundle_id, result, reason_codes, duration_ms}.

- Rollback restores prior behavior with verified receipt.
- Logs show deterministic diff/preview outputs.","notes":"[AlienArtifactPractical]

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-3mb","title":"fcp.twilio: Programmable Video API","description":"# fcp.twilio: Programmable Video API (FCP2)

## Goal
Provide a minimal, safe surface over Twilio Programmable Video:
- create/manage rooms
- list participants
- end rooms
- optionally fetch recordings

Video is high-cost and high-privacy. Treat most write operations as approval-gated.

## Scope
### Rooms
- Create room with constrained options (max participants, timeout).
- List rooms and get room status.
- End/complete a room.

### Participants
- List participants in a room.
- Optional: disconnect participant (dangerous).

### Recordings (optional; dangerous)
- List recordings for a room.
- Fetch recording metadata.

## Capability model (illustrative)
- `twilio.video.rooms.read`
- `twilio.video.rooms.write` (dangerous)
- `twilio.video.participants.read`
- `twilio.video.participants.write` (dangerous)
- `twilio.video.recordings.read` (dangerous)

## FCP2 requirements
- Single-zone binding.
- NetworkConstraints pinned to Twilio API host(s).
- For any write:
  - require approval when policy dictates
  - emit receipts + audit events with room SID and action
- Strict privacy hygiene:
  - never log participant identities or media URLs

## Deliverables
- Operation schemas:
  - `twilio.video.room.create`
  - `twilio.video.room.get`
  - `twilio.video.room.list`
  - `twilio.video.room.end`
  - `twilio.video.room.participants`
  - Optional: `twilio.video.recording.list`

## Test strategy (MUST)
- Mock-only tests for:
  - room lifecycle
  - approval gating
  - error mapping
  - log redaction

## Acceptance criteria
- Video operations are safe-by-default and auditable.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-3myr","title":"[SC] Attestation + SBOM object schemas","description":"# [SC] Attestation + SBOM object schemas

## Goal
Define canonical schemas and validation rules for SupplyChainAttestation and SBOM objects, including signature requirements and deterministic hashing.

## Scope
- SupplyChainAttestation schema (SLSA/in-toto style provenance) with binding to artifact digest.
- SBOM schema (CycloneDX/SPDX subset) describing components and dependencies.
- Signature requirements and trust-root binding rules.
- Canonical encoding and hash rules (stable across platforms).

## Deliverables
- Schema IDs and versions for:
  - SupplyChainAttestation: subject digest, predicate type, builder identity, buildType, materials, metadata (timestamps), slsa_level, provenance_hash.
  - SBOM: bom_format, bom_version, tool chain, component list (name, version, hashes, licenses), dependency graph.
- Canonical encoding rules (CBOR canonical + JSON canonical) and hashing algorithm selection.
- Validation rules: required fields, allowed predicate types, builder allowlist hooks.

## Tests
- Golden vectors for valid/invalid attestations and SBOMs.
- Deterministic hash tests (same semantic input -> same hash).
- Schema rejection tests for missing/extra fields.

## Logging Requirements
- Structured JSON logs for schema validation and hash determinism checks:
  - {test, schema_id, vector_id, result, duration_ms}.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Schemas are versioned, deterministic, and validated by conformance tooling.
- Encoding + hash outputs are stable across runs/OSes.
- Invalid/unsigned objects fail closed with clear error codes.","notes":"[AlienArtifactPractical]
- Add canonicalization proof tests: permute fields -> canonical bytes identical.
- Add deterministic vector generator with fixed seeds and algebraic edge cases.
[ExtremeOptimizationPractical]
- Batch ed25519 verification and reuse hashers where safe.
- Avoid allocations in canonicalization using stack buffers.

## Goal
Establish real-component unit/integration coverage (no mocks/fakes) plus a complete E2E script suite with rich structured logging.

## Definition: No mocks/fakes
- No mocking frameworks (wiremock), stub servers, or fake clients in the target suites.
- Tests exercise real code paths (actual protocol parsing, crypto, stores, mesh/session logic, sandbox enforcement).
- For external services (LLM, social APIs), tests must use dedicated sandbox accounts and real HTTP calls OR be marked manual-only with secrets gating.

## Deliverables
- Coverage inventory + gap map.
- Real-component test suites for core crates, mesh, store/repair, host+connector subprocess, sandbox/egress.
- E2E logging schema + validator + expanded scenario runner.

## Non-goals
- Do not delete existing mock-based tests; instead add real-component suites in parallel.
- No reliance on public production accounts.

## Success Criteria
- A coherent dependency graph of tasks that yields: (1) real-component unit/integration coverage, (2) comprehensive E2E scripts with machine-parseable logs.

+{"id":"flywheel_connectors-3p99","title":"TEST: No-mock coverage + full E2E logging (FCP2)","description":"# TEST: No-mock coverage + full E2E logging (FCP2)

## Goal
Establish real-component unit/integration coverage (no mocks/fakes) plus a complete E2E script suite with rich structured logging.

## Definition: No mocks/fakes
- No mocking frameworks (wiremock), stub servers, or fake clients in the target suites.
- Tests exercise real code paths (actual protocol parsing, crypto, stores, mesh/session logic, sandbox enforcement).
- For external services (LLM, social APIs), tests must use dedicated sandbox accounts and real HTTP calls OR be marked manual-only with secrets gating.

## Deliverables
- Coverage inventory + gap map.
- Real-component test suites for core crates, mesh, store/repair, host+connector subprocess, sandbox/egress.
- E2E logging schema + validator + expanded scenario runner.

## Non-goals
- Do not delete existing mock-based tests; instead add real-component suites in parallel.
- No reliance on public production accounts.

## Success Criteria
- A coherent dependency graph of tasks that yields: (1) real-component unit/integration coverage, (2) comprehensive E2E scripts with machine-parseable logs.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-3pdj","title":"[IDEA-19] Structured Log Redaction + Secret Scanner","description":"## Background
Logging standards forbid secrets/PII, but enforcement is inconsistent. A mechanical scanner reduces risk and enforces compliance across tests and CI artifacts.

## Goals
- Provide a reusable log scanner that detects secrets/PII patterns in JSONL logs.
- Integrate with test harnesses and compliance runner.
- Allow explicit allowlists for safe test fixtures.

## Non-goals
- Replacing runtime redaction; this is a verification layer.

## Approach
- Add a `LogRedactionScanner` in `fcp-testkit` or `fcp-conformance`.
- Detect common secret formats (JWTs, API keys, auth headers, base64 blobs) and obvious PII.
- Expose CLI for scanning artifacts.

## Risks

### 2. Canonical Serialization Tests

```rust
#[cfg(test)]
mod canonical_serialization_tests {
    use fcp_cbor::{CanonicalSerializer, SchemaId, to_canonical_cbor};
    use serde::{Deserialize, Serialize};
    use semver::Version;
    use std::collections::BTreeMap;

    #[derive(Serialize, Deserialize, PartialEq, Debug)]
    struct TestObject {
        id: u64,
        name: String,
        enabled: bool,
    }

    #[test]
    fn test_canonical_cbor_deterministic() {
        let obj = TestObject {
            id: 42,
            name: "test".to_string(),
            enabled: true,
        };

        let bytes1 = to_canonical_cbor(&obj).unwrap();
        let bytes2 = to_canonical_cbor(&obj).unwrap();

        assert_eq\!(bytes1, bytes2, "Same object must produce identical bytes");
    }

    #[test]
    fn test_canonical_cbor_map_key_ordering() {
        // Maps must have keys in lexicographic order (RFC 8949 4.2.1)
        let mut map = BTreeMap::new();
        map.insert("zebra", 1);
        map.insert("apple", 2);
        map.insert("mango", 3);

        let bytes = to_canonical_cbor(&map).unwrap();

        // Decode and verify key order
        let decoded: ciborium::Value = ciborium::from_reader(&bytes[..]).unwrap();
        if let ciborium::Value::Map(entries) = decoded {
            let keys: Vec<_> = entries.iter()
                .map(|(k, _)| k.as_text().unwrap())
                .collect();
            assert_eq\!(keys, vec\!["apple", "mango", "zebra"]);
        }
    }

    #[test]
    fn test_serializer_includes_schema_prefix() {
        let obj = TestObject { id: 1, name: "test".into(), enabled: true };
        let schema = SchemaId::new("fcp.test", "TestObject", Version::new(1, 0, 0));

        let bytes = CanonicalSerializer::serialize(&obj, &schema).unwrap();

        // First 32 bytes should be schema hash
        let prefix = &bytes[..32];
        assert_eq\!(prefix, schema.hash().as_bytes());
    }

    #[test]
    fn test_serializer_roundtrip() {
        let obj = TestObject { id: 123, name: "roundtrip".into(), enabled: false };
        let schema = SchemaId::new("fcp.test", "TestObject", Version::new(1, 0, 0));

        let bytes = CanonicalSerializer::serialize(&obj, &schema).unwrap();
        let decoded: TestObject = CanonicalSerializer::deserialize(&bytes, &schema).unwrap();

        assert_eq\!(obj, decoded);
    }

    #[test]
    fn test_deserialize_wrong_schema_fails() {
        let obj = TestObject { id: 1, name: "test".into(), enabled: true };
        let schema_v1 = SchemaId::new("fcp.test", "TestObject", Version::new(1, 0, 0));
        let schema_v2 = SchemaId::new("fcp.test", "TestObject", Version::new(2, 0, 0));

        let bytes = CanonicalSerializer::serialize(&obj, &schema_v1).unwrap();

        // Deserialize with wrong schema should fail
        let result: Result<TestObject, _> = CanonicalSerializer::deserialize(&bytes, &schema_v2);
        assert\!(result.is_err());
    }
}
```

### 3. Integer Encoding Tests (RFC 8949)

```rust
#[cfg(test)]
mod integer_encoding_tests {
    use fcp_cbor::to_canonical_cbor;

    #[test]
    fn test_smallest_integer_encoding() {
        // RFC 8949 requires smallest possible encoding
        // 0-23: single byte
        // 24-255: two bytes
        // 256-65535: three bytes
        // etc.

        let small = 23u64;
        let bytes = to_canonical_cbor(&small).unwrap();
        assert_eq\!(bytes.len(), 1); // 0x17

        let medium = 24u64;
        let bytes = to_canonical_cbor(&medium).unwrap();
        assert_eq\!(bytes.len(), 2); // 0x18 0x18

        let large = 256u64;
        let bytes = to_canonical_cbor(&large).unwrap();
        assert_eq\!(bytes.len(), 3); // 0x19 0x01 0x00
    }

    #[test]
    fn test_negative_integer_encoding() {
        let neg = -1i64;
        let bytes = to_canonical_cbor(&neg).unwrap();
        assert_eq\!(bytes, vec\![0x20]); // -1 encoded as major type 1
    }
}
```

        let id2 = ObjectId::compute(&obj2, &schema).unwrap();

    fn test_object_id_blake3_based() {
        let obj = DataObject { value: 123 };
        let schema = SchemaId::new("fcp.test", "DataObject", Version::new(1, 0, 0));

        let bytes = CanonicalSerializer::serialize(&obj, &schema).unwrap();

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-epqh","title":"FCP Connector Portfolio: Strategic Prioritization Framework","description":"# FCP Connector Portfolio: Strategic Prioritization Framework

## Goal
Define and maintain a rigorous prioritization framework for the connector portfolio (value, risk, effort, dependencies) so roadmap decisions are coherent, explainable, and user-focused.

## Executive Summary

This document captures the strategic thinking, prioritization rationale, and implementation roadmap for the Flywheel Connector Platform (FCP) connector portfolio. The goal is to maximize agent autonomy and capability while building a sustainable, high-quality connector ecosystem.

---

## Strategic Vision

### The Flywheel Effect
Connectors create a flywheel of value:
1. **More connectors** → Agents can do more things
2. **More capable agents** → More users adopt the platform
3. **More users** → More demand for connectors
4. **More demand** → Justification for building more connectors

### Design Principles
- **Quality over quantity**: Each connector should be production-ready, not a proof-of-concept
- **Ecosystem coherence**: Connectors should work together, enabling cross-service workflows
- **Security by default**: Default-deny, capability-based permissions throughout
- **Future-proof**: FCP2 spec compliance ensures consistency and upgradeability

---

## Prioritization Framework

### Value Assessment Model
Each connector is scored on five dimensions:

| Dimension | Weight | Meaning |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| **User Reach** | 25% | How many users need this service |
| **Agent Autonomy** | 25% | How much more can agents do with this |
| **Flywheel Synergy** | 20% | Integration potential with other connectors |
| **Strategic Position** | 15% | Competitive differentiation |
| **Implementation Cost** | 15% | Effort vs. payoff ratio |

### Tier Definitions

#### Tier 1: Critical Infrastructure (Score 93-100)
**Build immediately. Foundation for everything else.**

These connectors either:
- Enable the core agent workflow loop
- Provide capabilities no other connector can substitute
- Have massive user reach (>50% of target users)

Connectors:
- `fcp.twitter` (98) — Real-time social layer, high visibility
- `fcp.linear` (97) — Modern issue tracking, Beads sync
- `fcp.stripe` (96) — Payments/billing, revenue operations
- `fcp.youtube` (95) — Video/transcript access, research
- `fcp.browser` (95) — Universal fallback, any website
- `fcp.plaid` (94) — Banking/financial data
- `fcp.anthropic` (94) — Core AI provider
- `fcp.openai` (94) — Broadest AI capabilities
- `fcp.google-ai` (94) — Massive context, multimodal
- `fcp.vectordb` (93) — Memory/search infrastructure

#### Tier 2: High-Value Productivity (Score 83-92)
**Build in phase 2. Major capability expansion.**

These connectors:
- Serve large user segments
- Enable important workflow categories
- Have strong synergies with Tier 1

Connectors:
- `fcp.microsoft365` (91) — Enterprise productivity suite
- `fcp.jira` (89) — Enterprise issue tracking
- `fcp.twilio` (88) — Communications infrastructure
- `fcp.hubspot` (87) — CRM/marketing automation
- `fcp.airtable` (86) — No-code database
- `fcp.figma` (85) — Design collaboration
- `fcp.sentry` (85) — Error tracking/debugging
- `fcp.datadog` (84) — Observability platform
- `fcp.grafana` (83) — Open-source observability

#### Tier 3: Specialized Value (Score 70-82)
**Build in phase 3. Domain-specific capabilities.**

These connectors:
- Serve specific user segments very well
- Enable specialized workflows
- May be safety-critical (credentials, infrastructure)

Connectors:
- `fcp.kubernetes` (82) — Container orchestration
- `fcp.s3` (81) — Object storage
- `fcp.terraform` (80) — Infrastructure as code
- `fcp.1password` (79) — Credential management
- `fcp.arxiv` (78) — Research papers
- `fcp.zendesk` (77) — Customer support
- `fcp.semanticscholar` (77) — Citation analysis
- `fcp.annas-archive` (76) — Book/document search
- `fcp.reddit` (76) — Community discussions
- `fcp.docusign` (75) — E-signatures
- `fcp.homeassistant` (74) — Home automation
- `fcp.spotify` (72) — Music/podcast

#### Meta-Connectors: Force Multipliers
**Build throughout. Infrastructure for the infrastructure.**

These aren't service connectors but platform capabilities:
- `fcp.webhook-receiver` (88) — Inbound event gateway
- `fcp.cron` (86) — Scheduled task runner
- `fcp.mcp-bridge` — MCP server passthrough

---

## Flywheel Ecosystem Synergies

### Synergy Map

```
                    Agent Mail (coordination hub)
                           ↑
         ┌─────────────────┼─────────────────┐
         ↓                 ↓                 ↓
    AI Providers     Data Sources      Communication
    (Anthropic,      (GitHub,          (Slack, Twilio,
     OpenAI,          Linear,           Discord,
     Google AI)       Stripe)           Email)
         ↓                 ↓                 ↓
         └─────────────────┼─────────────────┘
                           ↓
    ┌──────────────────────┼──────────────────────┐
    ↓                      ↓                      ↓
 Vector DB            CASS/CM                   BV
 (memory)          (search/memory)       (task tracking)
```

### Key Integration Patterns

#### 1. Development Workflow
```
GitHub PR → Linear issue → Agent investigates →
  → Sentry errors + Datadog metrics →
  → Agent proposes fix → PR merged → Linear updated
```

#### 2. Customer Success Workflow
```
Zendesk ticket → Agent analyzes →
  → Searches knowledge base → Drafts response →
  → If complex: creates Linear issue →
  → Updates HubSpot contact record
```

#### 3. Revenue Operations Workflow
```
Stripe payment event → Agent correlates →
  → Updates HubSpot deal → Sends Twilio notification →
  → If failed: creates Zendesk ticket →
  → Generates report in Airtable
```

#### 4. Research Workflow
```
User question → Agent searches ArXiv + Semantic Scholar →
  → Downloads papers → AI summarizes →
  → Stores in Vector DB → Returns synthesis
```

---

## Implementation Roadmap

### Phase 1: Foundation (Weeks 1-4)
**Focus: Core agent capabilities**

- AI Providers: Anthropic, OpenAI, Google AI
- Meta: Webhook Receiver, Cron
- Critical: Twitter, Linear, GitHub
- Storage: Vector DB, S3

### Phase 2: Productivity (Weeks 5-8)
**Focus: Business workflows**

- Enterprise: Microsoft 365, Jira
- Communication: Twilio, Slack
- CRM: HubSpot
- Observability: Sentry, Datadog, Grafana
- No-code: Airtable
- Design: Figma
- Payments: Stripe, Plaid

### Phase 3: Specialization (Weeks 9-12)
**Focus: Domain expertise**

- Infrastructure: Kubernetes, Terraform
- Research: ArXiv, Semantic Scholar, Anna's Archive
- Support: Zendesk
- Legal: DocuSign
- Security: 1Password

### Phase 4: Expansion (Weeks 13+)
**Focus: Long-tail value**

- Social: Reddit
- Media: Spotify, YouTube
- Home: Home Assistant
- Additional based on demand

---

## Quality Standards

### Every Connector Must:
1. **Pass FCP2 compliance runner** — Static and dynamic checks
2. **Have no real API calls in tests** — Mock-only unit tests
3. **Implement proper zones** — Capability-based security
4. **Support audit logging** — All operations tracked
5. **Handle rate limits** — Respect service quotas
6. **Document synergies** — Integration patterns explicit

### Bead Standards
Each connector bead must include:
- Strategic value score with justification
- Concrete agent use cases (not abstract)
- Flywheel synergies with specific connectors
- Zone architecture recommendations
- Capability model (read/write/admin)
- Representative operations with examples
- Testing approach
- Acceptance criteria

---

## Risk Considerations

### API Stability
- Services may change APIs
- Version pinning where possible
- Deprecation monitoring needed

### Rate Limits
- Different per service
- Budget enforcement at zone level
- Queue management for bursts

### Security
- Credentials never written to disk
- 1Password as credential source
- Audit trails mandatory

### Cost
- Some APIs charge per call
- AI providers charge per token
- Budget controls essential

---

## Success Metrics

### Coverage Metrics
- % of target workflows enabled
- # of connectors at each tier
- Cross-connector workflow coverage

### Quality Metrics
- FCP2 compliance rate
- Test coverage per connector
- API error rates

### Usage Metrics
- Operations per connector
- Workflow completion rates
- User satisfaction scores

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-eqb","title":"fcp.homeassistant: Event subscription and real-time updates","description":"# fcp.homeassistant: Event subscription and real-time updates (FCP2)

## Goal
Provide streaming access to Home Assistant’s event bus via WebSocket:
- state_changed
- call_service
- custom events

## Scope
- Establish websocket session and authenticate.
- Subscribe to events with filters (domain/entity allowlists).
- Deliver events as FCP2 event envelopes with:
  - stable event IDs
  - correlation IDs

## Capability model (illustrative)
- `ha.events.subscribe` (sensitive)

## FCP2 requirements
- Single-zone binding.
- NetworkConstraints pinned (WS + HTTPS to HA host only).
- Streaming must be bounded/backpressured:
  - cap queue depth
  - drop/deny with explicit reason codes when overloaded
- Taint:
  - event payloads are tainted external input

## Deliverables
- Operation schema:
  - `ha.events.subscribe`
- Event envelope types:
  - `ha.event.state_changed`
  - `ha.event.call_service`

## Test strategy (MUST)
- Mock-only tests for:
  - reconnect + resubscribe
  - bounds/backpressure behavior
  - taint propagation

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-f3xi","title":"TEST: Offline Capability Unit Tests (Availability Tracking, Pre-staging, Coverage Metrics)","description":"# TEST: Offline Capability Unit Tests

## Goal
Comprehensive unit tests for offline capability tracking from V2 spec section 21.

## Test Categories

### OfflineAccess Tests
- can_access() returns true when local_symbols >= k
- can_access() returns false when local_symbols < k
- coverage() calculates correct ratio (local_symbols / k)
- Edge cases: k=0, local_symbols=0, overflow

### OfflineCapability Tests
- Accessible map tracks multiple objects correctly
- Object removal updates capability state
- Query by zone returns correct subset
- Memory efficient for large object counts

### Pre-staging Tests
- Access pattern tracking updates correctly
- Frequent objects prioritized for pre-staging
- Pre-staging respects device storage limits
- Stale access patterns decay appropriately
- Pre-staging triggers before predicted access

### Integration with Stores
- Symbol store updates reflect in OfflineAccess
- Symbol eviction updates can_access() correctly
- Repair loop improves coverage metrics

## Golden Vectors
- Location: tests/golden/offline_capability/
- Include: OfflineAccess serialization, coverage calculations

## Logging Requirements (per 1n78.35)
All tests emit structured JSON:
{
  "timestamp": "RFC3339",
  "test": "can_access_sufficient_symbols",
  "object_id": "...",
  "local_symbols": 60,
  "k": 50,
  "expected_can_access": true,
  "result": "pass|fail"
}

## Fuzz Targets
- OfflineAccess from arbitrary symbol counts
- Pre-staging priority calculation

- All can_access/coverage calculations correct

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-f4sw","title":"[IDEA-25] RaptorQ Parameter Auto-Tuning + Benchmarks","description":"## Background
Symbol size and repair parameters strongly impact performance and MTU safety. Current defaults are static and may be suboptimal per platform/path.

## Goals
- Add auto-tuning for symbol size and repair symbol count.
- Provide benchmark suite to compare configurations.
- Emit recommended parameters for common MTUs (LAN/DERP).

## Non-goals
- Changing the canonical wire format; only tune parameters.

## Approach
- Add a bench harness in `fcp-raptorq` and `fcp-cli bench` integration.
- Record decode/encode time, memory, and overhead.
- Store recommended presets in config.

## Risks
- Overfitting to benchmarks; keep conservative defaults and allow override.

## Success Criteria
- Bench suite produces consistent metrics across runs.
- Recommended parameters reduce p99 latency without MTU regressions.

## Acceptance Criteria (Refined)
- Bench suite outputs p50/p95/p99 and memory usage JSON.
- Preset table covers LAN and DERP MTU defaults.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-f50","title":"fcp.reddit: User Posting and Commenting","description":"# fcp.reddit: User Posting and Commenting (FCP2)

## Goal
Enable controlled Reddit participation:
- submit posts
- submit comments
- edit/delete own content

These are real-world side effects and must be tightly capability scoped.

## Scope
- Submit post to a subreddit (text/link).
- Submit comment.
- Edit content.
- Delete content.

## Capability model (illustrative)
- `reddit.post.write` (dangerous)
- `reddit.comment.write` (dangerous)
- `reddit.content.edit` (dangerous)

Policy guidance:
- Posting should often require ApprovalToken depending on zone policy.

## FCP2 requirements
- Single-zone binding.
- NetworkConstraints pinned.
- Content safety:
  - outbound content must be treated as a “publication” action
  - receipts + audit events include subreddit + post/comment id
  - never log post bodies

## Deliverables
- Operation schemas:
  - `reddit.post.submit`
  - `reddit.comment.submit`
  - `reddit.content.edit`
  - `reddit.content.delete`

## Test strategy (MUST)
- Mock-only tests for:
  - request formation
  - approval gating
  - audit/receipt emission
  - error mapping (subreddit rules, forbidden)

## Acceptance criteria
- Write operations are mechanically authorized, explainable, and auditable.
